title: Return-to-Sport Testing After ACL Reconstruction: Which Criteria Actually Predict Re-Injury Risk?
meta_description: Evidence-based return-to-sport criteria for ACL reconstruction. Which tests predict re-injury and which don't? Critical evaluation of current protocols.
html_content:
Return-to-sport decisions after ACL reconstruction remain one of the most challenging aspects of sports physiotherapy. Despite decades of research, second ACL injury rates hover around 15-20% in young athletes, suggesting our current testing batteries may not be capturing the factors that truly predict re-injury risk. The question isn't whether we should test — it's whether we're testing the right things.

Current return-to-sport protocols typically focus on time-based criteria, basic strength thresholds, and hop test performance. But recent evidence suggests this approach misses critical elements of neuromuscular control, psychological readiness, and movement quality that may be more predictive of successful return to sport (van et al., 2016).

The Problem with Traditional Return-to-Sport Criteria

Most ACL return-to-sport protocols centre on achieving 90% limb symmetry for quadriceps strength and passing a battery of hop tests. These criteria sound objective, but they're built on surprisingly weak foundations. The 90% threshold, while widely adopted, emerged from studies comparing ACL-injured athletes to their uninjured limb — not to normative data or injury risk stratification.

More concerning is the focus on bilateral comparison rather than absolute capacity. An athlete who was weak bilaterally before injury can achieve 90% limb symmetry while still being substantially below sport-specific demands. This approach also assumes the uninjured limb provides a valid reference point, ignoring potential compensatory weakness or bilateral deficits common in athletes with ACL injury (Mayer et al., 2025).

Time-based criteria present similar issues. The traditional 6-9 month timeline reflects tissue healing rather than functional readiness. Some athletes demonstrate excellent neuromuscular control at 4-5 months, while others show persistent deficits at 12 months. Using calendar time as a primary criterion ignores this individual variation and may either rush underprepared athletes back to sport or unnecessarily delay ready ones.

Strength Testing: Beyond the 90% Rule

Quadriceps strength remains fundamental to ACL return-to-sport assessment, but the testing approach needs refinement. Isokinetic dynamometry at 60°/sec provides reliable strength data, but peak torque values don't capture the rapid force development required in sport. Rate of force development and eccentric strength capacity may be more relevant to injury prevention than traditional peak strength measures.

Recent evidence suggests eccentric-oriented strength training produces superior return-to-sport outcomes compared to conventional protocols (Stojanović et al., 2023). This finding challenges the emphasis on concentric strength testing and highlights the need for eccentric assessment protocols. Athletes must decelerate and control landing forces that can exceed 6-8 times body weight — a demand poorly reflected in standard strength testing.

Testing should also address strength endurance rather than single-effort maximum strength. Sport demands repeated powerful contractions with minimal recovery time. A player who demonstrates excellent peak quadriceps strength but shows rapid fatigue during repeated testing may not be prepared for match conditions.

Practical strength testing improvements include:

  • Rate of force development assessment during the first 200ms of contraction
  • Eccentric strength testing at functional speeds
  • Repeated strength testing protocols to assess fatigue resistance
  • Comparison to sport-specific normative data rather than bilateral limb comparison

Hop Testing: What the Numbers Actually Tell You

Hop tests remain popular because they're practical and seem to capture functional capacity. The single-hop, triple-hop, crossover hop, and 6-meter timed hop form the standard battery. But what do these tests actually measure, and how well do they predict re-injury risk?

Hop distance provides some indication of power production, but it doesn't assess movement quality during landing — arguably the more important factor for injury prevention. An athlete can achieve 95% limb symmetry for hop distance while demonstrating poor knee control, excessive hip adduction, or compensatory trunk movement during landing.

The focus on symmetry indices also creates the same issues seen in strength testing. Athletes often develop compensatory strategies that allow them to achieve similar distances bilaterally while using different movement patterns. Video analysis during hop testing reveals these qualitative differences that quantitative distance measures miss.

More sophisticated hop testing should include:

  • Movement quality scoring during landing phases
  • Assessment of knee valgus, hip control, and trunk stability
  • Reactive hop testing that includes unpredictable directional changes
  • Sport-specific hopping patterns rather than standardised tests

Psychological Readiness: The Overlooked Component

Psychological factors may be more predictive of return-to-sport success than physical test results. Fear of re-injury, confidence in the knee, and overall psychological readiness significantly influence both performance and actual re-injury risk. Yet most return-to-sport protocols include minimal psychological assessment (Nedder et al., 2025).

The ACL-Return to Sport after Injury (ACL-RSI) scale provides a validated tool for assessing psychological readiness, but it's underutilised in clinical practice. Athletes scoring below 76 on this scale show significantly higher rates of failed return to sport, regardless of their physical test performance.

Psychological readiness manifests in movement patterns during testing. Athletes with high kinesiophobia often demonstrate altered movement strategies — reduced knee flexion during landing, increased reliance on the uninvolved limb, or hesitant approach to dynamic tasks. These compensatory patterns persist even when strength and hop test numbers look adequate.

Clinical assessment should include:

  • Formal psychological readiness screening using validated tools
  • Observation of movement confidence during testing
  • Assessment of decision-making under pressure
  • Evaluation of willingness to fully commit to sport-specific movements

Movement Quality Assessment: The Missing Link

Current return-to-sport criteria focus heavily on what athletes can produce — how much force, how far they can jump — but pay insufficient attention to how they move. Movement quality assessment addresses the neuromuscular control deficits that persist long after strength and power have recovered.

The Landing Error Scoring System (LESS) provides a standardised approach to movement quality assessment during jump-landing tasks. Athletes demonstrating poor landing mechanics — excessive knee valgus, reduced hip and knee flexion, asymmetric landing patterns — show higher subsequent injury rates regardless of their performance on traditional tests.

Sport-specific movement assessment adds another layer of evaluation. A footballer needs different movement competencies than a basketball player or alpine skier. Generic hop tests may miss sport-specific deficits that become apparent during actual game movements.

Video analysis during testing provides objective documentation of movement quality and creates a baseline for comparison during follow-up assessments. Simple smartphone video can capture most relevant movement patterns when recorded from appropriate angles.

Evidence-Based Protocol Development

Developing effective return-to-sport protocols requires integration of multiple assessment domains rather than relying on isolated criteria. Current evidence suggests a multi-faceted approach combining physical capacity, movement quality, psychological readiness, and sport-specific demands provides better predictive value than traditional protocols (van et al., 2016).

The testing battery should be sport-specific rather than generic. A tennis player needs different assessment criteria than a rugby player. Loading patterns, movement demands, and injury mechanisms vary significantly between sports, and testing protocols should reflect these differences.

Timing of assessments also requires reconsideration. Single-point-in-time testing provides only a snapshot of readiness. Serial assessments throughout rehabilitation provide better insight into progress trajectory and readiness for sport return. Athletes showing consistent improvement across multiple testing sessions may be better prepared than those with isolated good test results.

Key protocol development principles include:

  • Multi-domain assessment combining physical, psychological, and movement quality measures
  • Sport-specific testing protocols that reflect actual game demands
  • Serial assessment rather than single-point evaluation
  • Objective movement quality assessment using standardised scoring systems
  • Integration of athlete-reported outcome measures with objective testing

Implementing Objective Return-to-Sport Assessment

Effective return-to-sport testing requires systematic implementation rather than ad-hoc application of individual tests. Clinics need standardised protocols that can be consistently applied across different therapists and patient populations. This consistency enables meaningful comparison of results and identification of athletes at higher risk for unsuccessful return to sport.

Documentation systems should capture both quantitative test results and qualitative observations about movement patterns, confidence, and test approach. This information proves valuable for both clinical decision-making and medicolegal documentation of return-to-sport readiness.

Regular protocol review ensures testing methods remain aligned with current evidence. Return-to-sport assessment represents a rapidly evolving field, and protocols should be updated as new predictive factors are identified and validated.

The goal isn't to create barriers to return to sport, but to ensure athletes are genuinely prepared for the demands they'll face. Objective assessment protocols provide the foundation for confident return-to-sport decisions that protect both athlete welfare and clinical decision-making.

References

  1. van Melick N, van Cingel RE, Brooijmans F et al. Evidence-based clinical practice update: practice guidelines for anterior cruciate ligament rehabilitation based on a systematic review and multidisciplinary consensus. British journal of sports medicine. 2016;50(24):1506-1515. PubMed
  2. Mayer MA, Deliso M, Hong IS et al. Rehabilitation and Return to Play Protocols After Anterior Cruciate Ligament Reconstruction in Soccer Players: A Systematic Review. The American journal of sports medicine. 2025;53(1):217-227. PubMed
  3. Stojanović MDM, Andrić N, Mikić M et al. Effects of Eccentric-Oriented Strength Training on Return to Sport Criteria in Late-Stage Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL)-Reconstructed Professional Team Sport Players. Medicina (Kaunas, Lithuania). 2023;59(6). PubMed
  4. Nedder VJ, Raju AG, Moyal AJ et al. Impact of Psychological Factors on Rehabilitation After Anterior Cruciate Ligament Reconstruction: A Systematic Review. Sports health. 2025;17(2):291-298. PubMed

faq.q: What's wrong with the 90% limb symmetry rule for ACL return-to-sport?
faq.a: The 90% threshold compares the injured limb to the uninjured limb rather than to normative data or sport-specific demands. Athletes can achieve 90% symmetry while still being substantially below the strength levels required for their sport, especially if they had bilateral weakness before injury.

faq.q: Which psychological factors predict successful return to sport after ACL reconstruction?
faq.a: Fear of re-injury, confidence in the knee, and overall psychological readiness significantly influence return-to-sport success. The ACL-Return to Sport after Injury scale is a validated tool, with scores below 76 associated with higher rates of failed return to sport regardless of physical test performance.

faq.q: How important is movement quality assessment compared to strength and hop test results?
faq.a: Movement quality may be more predictive of re-injury risk than traditional strength and hop test numbers. Athletes can achieve good strength and hop distance scores while demonstrating poor landing mechanics, excessive knee valgus, or compensatory movement patterns that increase injury risk.

original_topic: Return-to-Sport Testing After ACL Reconstruction: Which Criteria Actually Predict Re-Injury Risk?
